speaker: interviewer
text: I know somebody cuts the grass, but they're not going to wait.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm not going to worry about it. You can't quite--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not-- it's not in Wynmoor. It's--

speaker: interviewer
text: --1995. The interview is with Lola Kay. This is reel six. All right. You're on the train now with your future husband, George--

speaker: interviewee
text: I did know that he's gonna be a husband. Yeah-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nice. Well, I'm saying your future, because I know today he's your future husband. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: So finally, we reach down. I came in. My sister wasn't home. She was with a friend that we were brought up together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I'm home. I was so happy, you couldn't believe it. Because there I met, you know, that we brought up together. They were sort of related-- my sister, the one she got married-- like cousins. And she was in their house, and my sister was expecting already. And so was-- you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was married. She met her husband right after the liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: He survived in-- in Russia, in Siberia-- something like that. He was married before, my brother-in-law. But you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he lost his whole family there. And the one sister, she came to the States. My brother-in-law's father was sort of like a rabbi. He had a small shul-- Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and he used to go back and forth to Poland, Lemberg--

speaker: interviewee
text: --back and forth. And his older daughter, he brought here. So she-- he had family and a brother-in-law. So he had a big family here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So you saw your sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw my sister, and then-- [SIGHS] --there was a reunion that you wouldn't believe it. And I didn't mention nothing to her, absolutely nothing, that she used to walk away and walk away from me, walk away. But I stayed most of the time there. I didn't go back to, you know, Schwandorf.

speaker: interviewee
text: I only-- of course, I had a spare-- a room for myself there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I could have gone back and forth.

speaker: interviewee
text: But here, I-- I have all the people from-- from-- from--

speaker: interviewee
text: --back-- old friends from back home. It was-- it was like, you know, a new world opened for me. Now I-- I felt so good. And-- and there was start-- they didn't liked my husband very much, because he was not financially-- ever a future or anything. You know, he's a nice guy, you know, well educated, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: They were looking for better for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were a few prospects that I wasn't interested in it. [LAUGHTER] They were trying. They were already established with, you know--

speaker: interviewee
text: --wealth and everything. And I wasn't then interested in-- in material things. It didn't mean a thing. I said I have to live with the person. OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: And you and George were becoming closer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I liked-- I liked, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Comfortable when I had a conversation.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you finally decide to get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd-- I-- I didn't took long. That was-- it's gonna be 49 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- June 16. They were talking, and I still didn't agreed, because there were other people that my brother-in-law wanted--

speaker: interviewee
text: --that-- that they were in business with him, business people, and they were already, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: The one who could buy me a-- a diamond. He couldn't even buy me a dinner, nothing-- [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's what I chose. And that's what-- [SIGHS] --so they said, if that's what it is, we make a wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. And you got married where?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how much after you got married did you decide to come to the United States? How long?

speaker: interviewee
text: After? No, I was still-- in my mind, I didn't had anybody in the United States. And that wasn't on my mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you want to go to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: All I wanted-- I was going to school in art-- train me. OK? And-- to learn my trade-- and Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- they were-- they taught me how to make brassieres-- girls--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were how to use a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. How to use a machine--

speaker: interviewee
text: --because you had-- got to have a trade--

speaker: interviewee
text: --you know, if you go to Israel. That what I think it was in Israel called for maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you finally realize that--

speaker: interviewee
text: And teach-- in a course, Hebrew I know 'cause I went to--

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH], so that would be no problem. But I needed to-- to have a background, to know something.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK? So I would probably, if I would go to Israel, I would wind up in a kibbutz to work in a--

speaker: interviewee
text: I like the outdoors and everything. So I would like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I also like-- you know, I would probably continue, 'cause I took courses-- painting, oil painting. And I did a lot of this.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you decide to go to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: When-- after we were married. We were married about a year, I think. And then he still didn't had any-- any prospect.

speaker: interviewee
text: You got to have some money.

speaker: interviewee
text: You got to have something to go to Israel. You cannot go like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he didn't had that. So they found a uncle, two uncles, here in the States--

speaker: interviewee
text: --that they came before, after the second-- after the first world war, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: They run away from there. I don't know-- Uncle Morris and Uncle Vikor, and they had families here. So--

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York. They had their families. And then we had contact with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: --because he was single. But they didn't sponsored me--

speaker: interviewer
text: It cost too much to sponsor two people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was only six weeks old while I was waiting for-- to emigrate. And I was waiting there. So it happened. And she was born. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: I like the way you say it-- it happened. It just doesn't happen. It takes--

speaker: interviewee
text: It takes-- well, I didn't take-- [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: --in a way, I wanted her to-- Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: But while we were waiting to emigrate and the interviews for the, you know, CIA-- and not like today. They come, you know, without anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Get a boat, and you come. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Get a-- and-- and-- or a-- so we made out, you know, papers, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: --you wound up in Tennessee of all places. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Of all places. I wished-- I wished I would never came to New York. I enrolled my daughter the same way they did to me in a religious school. [SIGHS] I said to my daughter, after-- I'm-- after 40 years, you know, a boss that my husband worked in-- in Nashville--

speaker: interviewee
text: --he lives in Fort Lauderdale-- we saw in the a column, you know, in Gretna, he is the biggest philanthropy-- here in-- in--

speaker: interviewee
text: So he saw our name. He said, oh my God! We called up. Sure enough, he came to visit.

speaker: interviewee
text: His boss. He gave-- he gave my husband the first job--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you stayed in Tennessee how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stay in Tennessee six years.

speaker: interviewee
text: --on vacation-- [DRINKING] --to New York, because he had already a-- a big family-- his brother, his two uncles, cousins--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you came to New York for a visit?

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't go with him. Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't go. He had vacation.

speaker: interviewee
text: What he did is saw in advertised-- [LAUGHTER] --that they have a job what he was, you know--

speaker: interviewee
text: --trained for. And he can make double the money.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you wound up in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he wrote me and called that, you know, we have to pack up and go. I didn't wanna go. My daughter was already-- [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: --in-- in-- you know, she was in the nursery. She was in--

speaker: interviewee
text: --in kindergarten. She was in the first grade, you know, learning Hebrew, learning, you know, in a-- in a--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you went. You went to New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to New York. It was like a different world. He warned me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live in? Where did you live in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: If you wouldn't believe where I wind up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Would you believe-- East New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: --when-- when-- when the-- not then. It was all Jewish!

speaker: interviewee
text: When I got off the train, I smelled the gefilte fish and everything-- all Jewish. You know-- used to go to Fortunoff's by--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, one was on Pitkin Avenue. The other one was underneath the L.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. That-- that what it was. I was on--

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Now-- now it's a bad neighborhood. Then it was an all Jewish neighborhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were living in a ghetto!

speaker: interviewee
text: --you know, and doing, you know, crochet, you know--

speaker: interviewee
text: --or knit-- or making sweaters, or things. They didn't read-- read any book.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you lived in-- you lived there for how long? How long did you live in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: New York, I was in that [NON-ENGLISH] place--

speaker: interviewee
text: But there was a nice shul.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to a nice shul. And-- and--

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sister-- my sister was living there.

speaker: interviewee
text: When it-- actually, when-- why he decided, my sister had a-- a luncheonette, a candy store there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's why they wanted me there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- well, it was doctors, and dentists--

speaker: interviewee
text: --and everything-- nice people, I'm telling you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So why do you call it [NON-ENGLISH] place?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's high class. Don't. High polluting.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you left New York. You left New York, and you-- you moved to the high class Boston.

speaker: interviewee
text: Boston-- that I should never, never have left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you should never have left Boston?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a place that I enjoyed--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had, you know. And Brandeis University was like a home away from home. I took courses there, and things. It was really great. And my, you know-- [SIGHS] --when he came up with Florida, that was the end of my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's the beginning of a new life.

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you something. Where you have friends and family, I think there where--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's hard to make a change.

speaker: interviewee
text: --for you. If I would be in Israel, I think I would be happy even if I have nobody-- you know,

speaker: interviewee
text: --close friends or what. It's-- it's what I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's the country. It's the country

speaker: interviewee
text: --that my father strived to be, to live-- my whole family wanted to be.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. You want to see pictures of her? Or no?

speaker: interviewer
text: Later. Later. I wanna hear about her first. Tell me about her.

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter was a brilliant student. OK. She-- she was like-- she start-- I started her off-- seven kids I enrolled her seven kids in-- in Nashville. The rabbi came from the Yeshiva University. He graduated--

speaker: interviewee
text: --he came to Nashville-- to Nashville, Tennessee-- be a rabbi. And he started from scratch-- seven kids-- mine, Esther [NON-ENGLISH], and five American.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't have to give-- don't give me all seven. Oh, yes. I just want to know about your daughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was so-- you know, since she started to read when she was four years old. I was sitting in Nashville, and my neighbor came home from work. And I hold up the paper, and she read the whole headline-- the-- so he couldn't believe it. He walked over. Did you read to her? Did you taught her that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Did she memorize? I said, no! What I used to do is buy puzzles with alphabet and things, and she used to put the puzzles. And she learned the alphabet. From that, she taught herself how to read.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your daughter went on to become a lawyer?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter went to Georgetown University, you now, law school. She graduated with honors, and her [NON-ENGLISH]-- [LAUGHTER] --husband had a job in Birmingham, Alabama.

speaker: interviewee
text: From all the places that she was, you know--

speaker: interviewee
text: --and she had to go. So the-- the things she did there, the-- the judges--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you have a case. She had a murder case, OK? The judge tells you you wouldn't be able to handle a murder case or something, you know, for a woman. A man can. A woman--

speaker: interviewee
text: --over there. They're still not, you know up to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. They don't accept women for--

speaker: interviewee
text: don't-- no. They don't accept. OK. So-- and that was hard for her to, you know, run-- hunt for clients, you know, to chase ambulances or what.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, so let's-- let's come up to date. So she went back to school, and she--

speaker: interviewee
text: She changed. When they moved to here, to Maryland, she changed--

speaker: interviewee
text: --that the, you know-- the health, you know, business came up.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a very, very big this. And since her husband is associated, so she figured they were-- you know, she's not gonna have any problem to pay rent, to secretaries, and to chase ambulance. She's--

speaker: interviewer
text: So she went into the nursing.

speaker: interviewer
text: She went to the nursing field.

speaker: interviewee
text: If she made one month, OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then the next month, if she didn't had any clients so.

speaker: interviewee
text: She became a nurse. She took, you know, didn't took long, because--

speaker: interviewee
text: --educated she is. So to her, it just went--

speaker: interviewee
text: --just like this. And she is a-- a nurse. She went to be a nurse practitioner now. I think she-- she thinks she's gonna teach, you know, she's gonna get--

speaker: interviewer
text: And she gave you a beautiful granddaughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: She gave me two granddaughters and a grandson.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, three. And recently you went to North--

speaker: interviewee
text: She's have a bat mitzvah. I had the greatest pleasure when Jesse was bat mitzvah. She conducts-- every Saturday she conducts the-- the congregation-- every Saturday. And she's close with the rabbis. And-- and now, Brenda, the-- the younger one, is doing the same thing-- very fluent in-- and, you know-- not Hebrew-- but in the reading--

speaker: interviewee
text: --very, you know. They're not-- they're modern orthodox.

speaker: interviewee
text: They're not the religious kind of orthodox. Like my niece, she lives in Staten Island. She just married a rabbi. Her daughter married a rabbi, my niece's daughter. From the wedding, it was about-- it's gonna be two years Michelle is married. They had a wedding. There were 800 peoples. And the whole university-- Yeshiva University-- [LAUGHS] --was there. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me-- let me ask you the last question. You finally decided to tell this story--

speaker: interviewer
text: --a long story, and a very interesting story. Why did you decide now to record this and tell us?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] OK. The feel-- the way I feel, what's going on in this country, that there are some radical movement coming up and telling that it never happens. There was no crematorium. There was nothing like that happen. And if wouldn't be for Steven Spielberg, you know, to make to come out-- 'cause I never-- I never spoke about it. It was in-- in me inside, and I never had a chance to get out of, you know, my system, this-- never talk about-- nobody who wanted to listen. I covered my arm with a-- with long sleeves.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what the Germans did, they-- they committed the biggest crime there is, you know, in human history. And they're gonna deny a thing like this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Skinheads and whatnot-- every time I turn on the television, it just-- you know, my skin is-- is-- is just-- I just can't stand it!

speaker: interviewer
text: Now when we gonna die out, OK? We are dead, OK? Whoever's left, who's gonna continue? Who's gonna tell, you know? Who'll tell the story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who's gonna tell the story? In this-- this country, I don't know if-- if any American Jew realizes what I know what can happen. One person-- it just take one to do something like that, to alienate a whole human race of Jews. That's what he wanted to do? That was his-- his ambition.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, why? Because I'm a Jew? We have no rights? Thanks God that we have Israel. Now what's going on in Israel, I don't like either. Well, that's another story. But basically, you want this story to be shown--

speaker: interviewer
text: --to your-- to your grandchildren and to your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I want-- I never want her to forget.

speaker: interviewee
text: They shouldn't forget. My-- I know Jessica, she'll never forget, you know, my older granddaughter. She'll never forget. But she's gonna continue. I took my little grandson. He's eight years old. When I was in Washington, I had that interview--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I took him. And he watched the monitor. He watched, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that boy was-- how old was this boy? 10 years old. And he tells his story today what was going on when he was living in Germany. And he listened.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my tears were running. So he kept, you know, looking at the monitor. He looked at me, at the monitor, and me. His head kept going. I know the way that child felt, you know. He knows I was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he imagines things. He's so smart. He's got a report card he brought home-- my daughter called me-- outstanding.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's smart, but he said, you know, when we walked out, he said, grandma-- I'm not-- we wanted to continue. I had a interview. So I couldn't continue. I was already there once.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this time, when I went for Passover, I had this interview. And the, you know-- they gave me a interview, because they wanted to know about Anne Frank--

speaker: interviewer
text: And so that's something that-- don't you think that I should have told that story? Absolutely. And I think the most important fact is that when we're all gone, that this story will live on, and that your-- your grandchildren and your great-grandchildren will always know of you and where you were, and how you survived this-- this terrible part of history.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how-- how I survived. What really made me survive that I-- I said to my-- I kept saying to myself, I have to tell this story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I thought nobody else is gonna be alive but me. I'm gonna be the only one alive. So I have to tell this. I imagined in my mind--

speaker: interviewee
text: --that I'm the only one who's gonna-- then when I saw, oh, thanks God. So many people, you know. And then I met somebody from-- like I had told you, I went to New York, and I-- and my sister, when she was already, you know, married and-- and-- and second-- she was supposed to be-- now they live in Israel. Right away I think, he's gonna to be proud. They live in Israel. She would never move over. You know, one sister lives here, and the other one never moved from Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: I thank you very much for this interview. It's been a pleasure talking to you, and I'm sure--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm so glad I did, because it was important to me. If it's gonna do any good-- I hope so.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where you gonna show this? To nobody?

speaker: interviewer
text: No it will, it will go down, and people will see this. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I'll explain that to you later. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: The thing is that I wanted to know the Germans I was there! Like I spoke in the, you know, in-- in Gifford University.

speaker: interviewee
text: My granddaughter was there. I'm here-- alive. I'm telling you I was there. Those kids never saw anybody, you know, in flesh and blood alive. They might see something on television, which they're not interested or read, or what. The professor couldn't get through them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you're being there, I think they-- they saw a living--

speaker: interviewee
text: So I think I made a different, and those kids were ecstatic. They were just unbelievable. The whole campus was in one place, and I'm gonna do it again. I'm gonna speak out wherever I have a chance.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about the-- the number.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Shall I? My number is 39940. And I had this tattoo in Birkenau. When we first arrived, that's what they did. They shaved your head, and they dehumanized you. And you weren't human anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this, number to me, is a reminder-- is for the rest of my life. I asked why I don't take that number out. I said, I'm not ashamed, because the people that did that to me, they should be ashamed what they did. And that's it. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What is it? Who is it? Who is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this is my sister's-- my oldest sister's firstborn, two years old. So I think that was in 1942. My sister was nine months pregnant with her second child already. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: She was-- the last time she was wandering around.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did she die? What happened to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was wandering around, and the SS saw a child wandering down. There was a cut down three, and they took her, you know, they took a shot. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] OK. That-- that's me after the liberation in Schwandorf. I took courses, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Just tell me what this is.

speaker: interviewee
text: --a picture of me. That's like a passport.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK? This is-- I'm a live member in ORT. So we needed pictures in order. I thought I'm gonna go to Israel. So this is like a passport. No matter where I go, I have to take this with me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my daughter, Anne. And she's in that picture-- she is about 18 years old. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Those are my three grandchildren-- Jessica, Brenda, and Sam. One is 19, one is 14, and one is 8.
